metadata.title: Research Mutational Signatures: What Can a Tumor Genome Tell You About Exposure History?

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Shalom, Liam — in for Bear. Mutational signatures — reading the past? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.segment: Research Mutational Signatures: What Can a

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Mutational signatures — reading the past.

A lung adenocarcinoma in a never-smoker has the APOBEC signature instead of the tobacco G-to-T signature. The genome records what happened to the cell. What that recording tells you about etiology, prognosis, and treatment — and how reliable the inference is — is the question.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- problem card: never-smoker lung cancer with APOBEC signature; genome as historical record

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to describe five major COSMIC mutational signatures with actionability data.

props.command:
claude "Describe five major mutational signatures using
the COSMIC catalog: SBS4 (tobacco), SBS7a/b (UV),
SBS2/13 (APOBEC), SBS6 (MMR-deficiency), SBS22
(aflatoxin). For each: dominant mutation spectrum,
which cancers carry it, what generates it, and does
identifying it currently change clinical management?"

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) -- Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the code. The script formats Claude's five-signature response into a tabular display with an actionability column — so the clinical utility of each signature is immediately visible.

props.code:
# signature_table.py -- Claude Code output
SIGS = [
  {"id": "SBS4",  "exposure": "tobacco",
   "cancers": "lung", "actionable": "NO"},
  {"id": "SBS7",  "exposure": "UV light",
   "cancers": "melanoma", "actionable": "YES - sun protection"},
  {"id": "SBS2/13", "exposure": "APOBEC",
   "cancers": "breast, lung, bladder", "actionable": "EMERGING"},
  {"id": "SBS6",  "exposure": "MMR defect",
   "cancers": "CRC, endometrial", "actionable": "YES - pembrolizumab"},
  {"id": "SBS22", "exposure": "aflatoxin",
   "cancers": "HCC", "actionable": "NO - retrospective only"},
]
print(f"{'Sig':<8} {'Exposure':<12} {'Cancers':<22} Actionable")
print("-" * 60)
for s in SIGS:
  print(f"{s['id']:<8} {s['exposure']:<12} {s['cancers']:<22} {s['actionable']}")

beats.narration_text: Run it. Five bars growing upward from the baseline. SBS4 tobacco — tall bar in INK. SBS7 UV light. SBS2/13 APOBEC. SBS6 MMR defect in CRIMSON — this is the clinically actionable one. SBS22 aflatoxin. Each bar labeled with its exposure etiology as it rises.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: five labeled bars growing upward; SBS6 in CRIMSON; exposure labels beside each bar

beats.narration_text: Now ask for the clinical case where signature analysis actually changed a treatment plan.

props.command:
claude "Find a documented case or published report
where mutational signature analysis changed a
patient's treatment plan -- for example a
never-smoker found to carry SBS4, or MSI-high
identified via SBS6 leading to pembrolizumab
eligibility."

beats.narration_text: The clinical annotation. The same five bars, but now a CRIMSON callout on SBS6: MSI-high detected via MMR deficiency signature — pembrolizumab eligible. This is the tumor-agnostic approval pathway. SBS6 is not just a label; it is a clinical trigger.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: same five bars + CRIMSON callout on SBS6: MSI-high -> pembrolizumab eligible

beats.narration_text: Signatures read the past but are imperfect predictors of the future. They tell you what happened to the cell — which mutational process was active. They do not tell you which current oncogene is driving it, or whether the exposure is still ongoing. Of the five major signatures, only one — SBS6 — is directly clinically actionable.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: past vs future distinction; SBS6 as only directly actionable

beats.narration_text: Look up your tumor type in the COSMIC mutational signatures portal and verify which signatures are dominant. If SBS6 appears, MSI testing is clinically actionable — pembrolizumab eligibility depends on it. If SBS4 appears in a never-smoker, the implication is secondhand exposure — the clinical management does not change, but the etiology question does.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps card: check COSMIC portal, SBS6 trigger for MSI testing

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Signatures read the past but are imperfect predictors of the future. They tell you what happened to the cell — which mutational process was active. They do not tell you which current oncogene is driving it, or whether the exposure is still ongoing. Of the five major signatures, only one — SBS6 — is directly clinically actionable. Look up your tumor type in the COSMIC mutational signatures portal and verify which signatures are dominant. If SBS6 appears, MSI testing is clinically actionable — pembrolizumab eligibility depends on it. If SBS4 appears in a never-smoker, the implication is secondhand exposure — the clinical management does not change, but the etiology question does.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research Mutational Signatures: What Can a Tumor Genome Tell You About] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
